mnemonics.title: Negation Flip Rule
mnemonics.content: When negating quantifiers, flip the symbol and negate the predicate: ¬∀ becomes ∃¬, and ¬∃ becomes ∀¬. Remember: 'All' becomes 'Not all' (some don't), 'Some' becomes 'None'.

mnemonics.title: ∀ = All, ∃ = Exists (Some)
mnemonics.content: Think of ∀ as an upside-down 'A' meaning 'ALL' and ∃ as 'E' for 'Exists'. Just like A comes before E alphabetically, ALL comes before EXISTS in the negation cycle.

mnemonics.title: Scope is the 'Territory'
mnemonics.content: Quantifier scope is like a queen's territory in chess—everything the quantifier 'controls' or 'owns'. Whatever is inside its parentheses or brackets belongs to its scope.

acronyms.usage: Used to make statements about every element in the domain: ∀x P(x)
Used to make statements about at least one element: ∃x P(x)
Rule for negating nested quantifiers: Flip each quantifier type and add negation to each predicate

visualTips.visualization: Picture a large circle (the domain) with an arrow pointing to EVERY element inside, like a 'coverage' indicator showing all elements are included.
Picture a magnifying glass focusing on just ONE element in the circle, showing that at least one element satisfies the property.
Think of quantifiers as a pipeline: ∀x ∃y flows left-to-right (for each input x, produce output y), while ∃y ∀x means find ONE special y that works with ALL inputs.

analogies.analogy: Like a teacher saying 'Every student must submit homework'—it applies to ALL students in the class (domain).
Like saying 'Someone in this room has a blue shirt'—we only need ONE person to satisfy the statement.
A bound variable is like an employee working within a company (quantifier)—their role is defined by the company. A free variable is like a freelancer with no specific employer.
Like saying 'All my socks are purple'—if I have no socks, the statement is technically true (vacuously) because there's no sock to disprove it.
